items.text: ParentClosePolicy enum with variants RequestCancel, Terminate, and Abandon is defined in crates/aion-core/src/event.rs with derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize) and doc comments explaining each variant's semantics.
ParentClosePolicy is re-exported from crates/aion-core/src/lib.rs so downstream crates (aion, aion-server) import it as aion_core::ParentClosePolicy.
Event::ChildWorkflowStarted gains a policy: ParentClosePolicy field, recorded alongside child_workflow_id, workflow_type, input, and package_version.
Command::SpawnChild in crates/aion/src/durability/command.rs gains a policy: ParentClosePolicy field alongside key, workflow_type, and input.
record_child_workflow_started in crates/aion/src/durability/recorder.rs gains a policy: ParentClosePolicy parameter and threads it into the Event::ChildWorkflowStarted it appends.
resolve_or_execute_live in crates/aion/src/durability/executor.rs threads the policy from Command::SpawnChild through to record_child_workflow_started after a live spawn.

The resolver in crates/aion/src/durability/resolver.rs passes through the policy field when replaying a ChildWorkflowStarted from recorded history, preserving it for any downstream consumer that inspects the replayed event.

CorrelationKey derivation in crates/aion/src/durability/correlation.rs is unchanged: the policy field on ChildWorkflowStarted does not alter the Child(ordinal) correlation key because correlation is by spawn position, not by policy.

When handle_process_exit_async in crates/aion/src/lifecycle/completion.rs records a fresh terminal (Completed, Failed, or the monitor-failure error arm), it invokes a propagation step that walks the parent's ChildWorkflowStarted events and dispatches each child's recorded ParentClosePolicy.
When a workflow is cancelled (WorkflowCancelled terminal), the same propagation step is invoked, walking children and dispatching their policies.
When a workflow times out (WorkflowTimedOut terminal), the same propagation step is invoked.

RequestCancel policy dispatches a cancellation request to the child workflow through the existing cancellation path (the same mechanism used by explicit cancel requests), letting the child handle it gracefully.

Terminate policy records an immediate WorkflowFailed terminal for the child through the child's Recorder (invariant 3: single writer) with a message indicating parent-policy termination, and notifies the child's completion subscribers.
Abandon policy takes no action: the child continues running as if the parent had not terminated.
Propagation is recursive: a child terminated or cancelled by its parent's policy triggers the child's own children's policies, walking the descendant tree until all edges are resolved.
A child that has already reached a terminal state (its own completion, failure, cancellation, or timeout) is skipped by propagation: the policy is not re-applied to an already-terminal child.

During startup recovery in crates/aion/src/durability/recovery.rs, after recovering a workflow whose history contains a terminal event, the recovery step walks the workflow's ChildWorkflowStarted events and checks whether each child's policy has been applied (the child has reached a terminal or the Abandon policy was selected). Unapplied policies are dispatched.

The recovery re-arm reads the policy from the recorded ChildWorkflowStarted event in the parent's history, not from any configuration or runtime state, preserving the determinism boundary (invariant 2).
If multiple children have pending propagations, recovery dispatches them in the same ChildWorkflowStarted recording order (deterministic, reproducible).

child.spawn in gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/child.gleam gains a required policy parameter (a Gleam type representing the three variants) before the codec parameters, and spawn_config encodes the policy into the JSON config passed to the NIF boundary.
child.spawn_and_wait in gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/child.gleam gains the same required policy parameter, forwarded to spawn.
workflow.spawn and workflow.spawn_and_wait in gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/workflow.gleam gain the required policy parameter, forwarding to child.spawn and child.spawn_and_wait.
Both spawn_and_wait call sites in examples/stacked-dev/src/stacked_dev.gleam are updated with an explicit ParentClosePolicy argument.
The spawn_and_wait call site in examples/stacked-dev/src/dispatch.gleam is updated with an explicit ParentClosePolicy argument.

All child.spawn and child.spawn_and_wait call sites in the test fixtures (nested_chain level_one.gleam, level_two.gleam, level_self.gleam; child_query parent_query.gleam) are updated with explicit policy arguments.

An e2e test exercises all three policies: a parent that terminates with a RequestCancel child verifies the child receives a cancellation; a parent with a Terminate child verifies the child is immediately failed; a parent with an Abandon child verifies the child continues running after the parent's terminal.

No new Event variant is introduced (the policy is a field on ChildWorkflowStarted, not a new event); no new terminal status variant is added; status_from_events derives status from the same event types as before (invariant 4, CN1).
No EventStore::append call is added outside the Recorder; all terminal records for policy-terminated children go through the child's Recorder under its lock (invariant 3, CN2).
No #[allow], #[expect], unwrap, or expect appears in non-test library code added by any brief; every touched source file stays under 500 LOC excluding tests (CN6).
cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, and the full workspace test suite pass clean at the cluster's landing commit.
No arbitrary default for the policy: omitting the policy parameter is a compile-time error in both Gleam and Rust (CN4, ADR-001).
